1: The date — “twelfth year, first day of the twelfth month” = March 3, 585 BCE — places this lament approximately 6-7 months after Jerusalem's fall (the city fell in the fourth/fifth month of the eleventh year). The news of Jerusalem's fall reached Ezekiel in the tenth month of the twelfth year (Ezekiel 33:21 = January 585 BCE), but this oracle in the twelfth month comes 2-3 months after that devastating news. The dirge over Egypt is thus composed in the period immediately after Ezekiel learned definitively of Jerusalem's destruction — giving it a particular emotional weight. The prophet who mourned Jerusalem's fate is now pronouncing a formal lament over the empire that failed to save her.
2: The opening double image — “young lion among the nations” and “like a monster in the seas” (vetannin bayamim) — combines Egypt's two royal emblems. The lion was the universal ancient symbol of royal power and military strength: the Sphinx, the Egyptian royal title “strong bull,” and the lion-headed goddess Sekhmet were all royal symbols. The sea monster (tannin) returns from Ezekiel 29:3, where Pharaoh was the great sea creature lying in the Nile. “You burst forth in your rivers and troubled the waters with your feet and muddied their rivers” — the image of a powerful creature disturbing the waters is the opposite of Egypt's self-presentation: Egypt identified itself with the Nile's life-giving order, but YHWH sees only turbulence and muddying.
3: “I will throw my net over you with a host of many peoples, and they will haul you up in my net” — YHWH as divine fisherman with a net (“garnet” imagery) is distinct from the fish-hook of Ezekiel 29:4. The net captures larger prey and is operated by multiple people (“host of many peoples” = the coalition of nations Nebuchadnezzar commanded). The Babylonian army was multiethnic: it included Chaldean, Aramean, Median, and various Levantine contingents. In Egyptian royal iconography, the king hunted fish and fowl with nets in the marshes — YHWH reverses this: the royal fisherman becomes the fish caught in YHWH's net.
4: “I will throw you on dry land and hurl you into the open field” — removal from water to dry land reverses the sea-creature's natural habitat, replicating Ezekiel 29:5 (Pharaoh thrown into the wilderness). The bird- and beast-feeding imagery echoes the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:26 and Jeremiah 7:33. “Fill all the beasts of the earth with you” — the great sea creature that once consumed others becomes food for all. The ecological reversal (predator becomes prey) is the natural expression of divine poetic justice: Egypt, which fed on weaker nations, is itself consumed.
5: “I will lay your flesh on the mountains and fill the valleys with your remains” — the scale of the carnage suggests a total defeat: the mountains and valleys of Egypt covered with flesh. In ancient siege warfare, mass casualties were left unburied on the battlefield; the landscape of defeat was literally covered with the dead. The specific topographic mention (mountains and valleys) in a relatively flat country like Egypt suggests that the imagery is trans-local — the mountains are the mountains of neighboring lands where Egyptian armies fell, not just Egypt's own terrain.
6: “I will drench with your blood the land where you swim, even to the mountains; the ravines will be filled with you” — the blood-imagery continues the reversal: the Nile waters that “made Egypt great” (Ezekiel 31:4) are now saturated with Pharaoh's blood. The Exodus connection is explicit: Exodus' first plague turned the Nile to blood (Exodus 7:14-24). Here the blood of Egypt's judgment again flows through the Nile system — the second Egypt judgment mirrors the first. The theological pattern of Exodus-judgment repeated in Babylonian-conquest judgment is characteristic of prophetic typology.
7: “When I blot you out I will cover the heavens and darken their stars; I will cover the sun with a cloud and the moon will not give its light” — cosmic darkness is the most dramatic theophanic sign. The sun, moon, and stars were Egyptian deities: Re (the sun), Thoth (associated with the moon), and the stars as divine council members. YHWH darkening the Egyptian sky is a direct attack on Egyptian theology — the gods of Egypt are extinguished. The same darkness-judgment appeared as Egypt's ninth plague (Exodus 10:21-23) and is applied eschatologically throughout the prophets (Isaiah 13:10 for Babylon; Joel 2:10, Ezekiel 3:15; Amos 8:9). The scale of cosmic darkness in response to Pharaoh's death expresses the world-historical significance of Egypt's fall.
8: “All the luminous lights of the sky I will darken over you” (kol-meorey or bashamayim achshichem aleykha) — the “luminous lights” include not only sun and moon but all the celestial bodies that regulated the Egyptian calendar, agricultural seasons, and religious festivals. Egyptian civilization was organized around precise solar observation (the solar calendar, the heliacal rising of Sirius heralding the Nile flood) — darkening these lights dismantles the Egyptian ordering of time and cosmos. “Bring darkness upon your land” — darkness in the land where light was most worshipped (the sun-cult of Re/Aten/Amun-Re) is the most targeted possible judgment.
9: “I will trouble the hearts of many peoples when I bring your ruin among the nations” — Egypt's fall was not a localized event; it reverberated through the international system. The “many peoples” whose hearts were troubled include Israel (who had relied on Egypt), the Cushites and Libyans (Egypt's southern and western neighbors), the remaining Phoenician city-states, and the smaller kingdoms of the Levant. “Countries you have not known” (aratsot asher lo-yedatam) — the news would travel to distant peoples who had never had direct contact with Egypt, carried by merchants, diplomats, and refugees.
10: “Their kings will shudder with horror when I brandish my sword before them; they will tremble continually, each man for his own life, on the day of your fall” — the neighboring kings' reaction to Egypt's fall is terror: if even Egypt falls, what are they? The phrase “brandish my sword before them” (henif charbi al-pneyhem) is YHWH as divine warrior displaying his weapon. In ANE royal iconography, the king brandishing weapons before enemies was the standard visual vocabulary of royal power; YHWH appropriates this imagery. The kings' trembling “each man for his own life” is the self-protective instinct of the vulnerable in the presence of overwhelming power.
11: “The sword of the king of Babylon will come against you” — after the extended metaphorical language of nets, fish, cosmic darkness, and sword-brandishing, the specific historical agent is named: Nebuchadnezzar. The transition from cosmic/metaphorical to specific/historical is characteristic of Ezekiel's prophetic technique: the cosmic frame interprets the historical event, and the historical event grounds the cosmic claim. Egypt's fall is simultaneously the fall of a chaos creature (Ezekiel 32:2-10) and the conquest of a specific nation by a specific king in a specific historical period.
12: “By the swords of the mighty I will cause your multitude to fall — the most ruthless of nations, all of them; they will destroy the pride of Egypt, and all its multitude will be wiped out” — “the most ruthless of nations” (Babylon's epithet in Ezekiel) will “destroy the pride of Egypt” (vishedu get Mitzrayim). The “pride” (gaon) of Egypt was its claim to be the premier world power — the “pride of strength” promised to Israel in covenant blessing (Leviticus 26:19 — which became a curse when forfeited through disobedience) is here Egypt's national self-claim. The wiping out of Egypt's multitude mirrors the exile of Israel's multitude — same vocabulary, same theological structure.
13: “I will destroy all its livestock from beside the great waters” — Egyptian cattle were grazed on the Nile Delta's rich flood plains. The annual inundation deposited fertile silt that supported enormous herds; the destruction of livestock means the destruction of Egypt's agricultural economy. “No human foot will trouble them anymore, nor will the hooves of cattle disturb them” — the stillness of the desolated waters echoes Ezekiel 32:2 (Pharaoh muddied the rivers with his feet) — what Pharaoh did is undone; the waters are still but because there are no living beings to disturb them.
14: “Then I will let their waters settle and cause their rivers to flow like oil” (az ashqia meymeyhem venahrotam kashemen olik) — the tranquil flowing oil-rivers are the image of peaceful waters after judgment has cleared away the turbulence. In the ancient world, olive oil was the symbol of blessing, luxury, and peace. The Nile flowing like oil is Egypt at peace — but a peace of desolation, not prosperity. The rivers flow smoothly because nothing disturbs them; the land is empty. This is the dreadful peace of total defeat, contrasting with the muddied waters of Pharaoh's pride (Ezekiel 32:2).
15: “When I make the land of Egypt desolate and the land is stripped of everything it was filled with” — the stripping of the land (veshomemotiy et-eretz Mitzrayim) corresponds to the covenant-curse of Leviticus 26:32-35 applied to Israel (the land “stripped” and “desolate” while Israel is exiled). Egypt receives the same desolation-curse that YHWH applied to his own covenant people. “Then they shall know that I am the LORD” — the final knowledge formula in the oracle. Even the desolation of Egypt is a means of YHWH's self-revelation: the greatest empire of the ancient world laid waste demonstrates that YHWH alone is LORD.
16: “This is the lamentation they will sing over her; the daughters of the nations will sing this lamentation over Egypt and all its multitude” — the oracle formally identifies itself as a qinah (lament/dirge). “The daughters of the nations” (benot hagoyim) are the female mourners of neighboring nations who will sing Egypt's funeral song — the professional mourner tradition (“skilled women” of Jeremiah 9:17; “daughters of music” in Qohelet Ezekiel 12:4) applied internationally. The nations mourn Egypt because Egypt's fall affects all of them — the loss of the great power leaves a void that all nations feel. Ezekiel's oracle itself is the qinah that the nations will sing.
17: The second date in Ezekiel 32 — “twelfth year, fifteenth of the month” = March 17, 585 BCE — places the Sheol-descent oracle only two weeks after the first lamentation (Ezekiel 32:1, March 3). These two oracles together form the conclusion of the Egypt collection: first a lament (Ezekiel 32:1-16), then a theological commentary on Egypt's place in the underworld gallery of fallen empires (Ezekiel 32:17-32). The Sheol descent section is the final of the six Egypt oracles and brings the collection to a theological climax: Egypt joins Assyria, Elam, and the other great powers in the underworld community of the dishonored dead.
18: “Son of man, wail for the multitude of Egypt and bring them down — both Egypt and the daughters of the majestic nations — to the depths of the earth, with those who go down to the Pit” — Ezekiel is commanded to perform a prophetic wail that functions as an escort-to-Sheol. The “daughters of the majestic nations” (banot hagoyim adirut) are the subject nations and allies of Egypt who will accompany her into the underworld. In ANE tradition, a great king's death was accompanied by the deaths of servants, horses, and retinue — here the entire Egyptian imperial system descends together. The “Pit” (bor) is Sheol, the underworld — the place of the dead.
19: “Whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and lie with the uncircumcised” — the rhetorical question challenges Egypt's self-image as the most beautiful and powerful of civilizations. Egyptian art and culture celebrated beauty (neferet, beautiful/good, is the root of “Nefertiti”) as a divine attribute; Egypt's temples, statues, and paintings are the most aesthetically sophisticated of the ancient world. “Go down and lie with the uncircumcised” — the command is the prophetic death-pronouncement; Sheol is the destination. “Uncircumcised” (arlim) in the context of Egypt is significant: Egypt practiced circumcision (attested in ancient Egyptian art and texts), so being associated with the uncircumcised is Egypt's ultimate ritual dishonor.
20: “They will fall among those slain by the sword; Egypt is delivered to the sword” — the passive “delivered” (nitna lecherev) places Egypt in the category of covenant-cursed nations: the same passive formula appears in Deuteronomy 1:27 (Israel feared YHWH had “delivered us into the hand of the Amorites”), Deuteronomy 20:13 (YHWH delivers cities into Israel's hand), and throughout Joshua for the nations given over to Israel. Now the formula is applied in reverse: YHWH delivers Egypt over to the sword. “Draw her down with all her multitude” (mashkuha vekol-hamoneha) — the prophetic command to drag Egypt into Sheol; Ezekiel's oracle is both the announcement and the execution of the sentence.
21: “The mightiest warriors will speak to him from the midst of Sheol” — in ANE underworld literature, the newly dead were greeted by the previously dead. The Babylonian Descent of Ishtar features the dead calling out to the goddess who has entered Sheol; the Akkadian Epic of Gilgamesh includes an underworld scene where Enkidu reports on the dead he encounters. Isaiah 14:9-10 has Sheol stirring to greet the fallen king of Babylon: “Even Sheol beneath is stirred up to meet you when you come ... all of them will respond and say to you: You too have become as weak as we!” Ezekiel's Sheol greeting follows the same literary convention.
22: Assyria in Sheol — “with all its company; their graves surround it — all of them slain, fallen by the sword.” Assyria fell with the destruction of Nineveh (612 BCE) by the combined Median and Babylonian forces. The Assyrian empire's fall was so sudden and complete that the subsequent generation found the ruins of Nineveh and could barely believe the mighty city had existed (Nahum and Zephaniah both record the astonishment at Nineveh's end). Assyria's “company” (kehalo) in Sheol — all the provincial armies, subject nations, and allied peoples who fell with it — is the ANE equivalent of a mass grave of empire.
23: “Its graves are in the depths of the Pit, its company gathered around its tomb — all slain, fallen by the sword, who had spread terror in the land of the living” — the phrase “spread terror in the land of the living” (yitnu chittitam beeretz hahayyim) is repeated eight times in Ezekiel 32:23-32 as the refrain of the Sheol gallery. It is the common description of every fallen empire's earthly impact — what these nations shared in life was their capacity to terrorize. In Sheol this terror is gone: the same nations lie together as equals, powerless, sharing only the shame of violent death far from homeland.
24: Elam — “with all its multitude around its tomb — all slain, fallen by the sword, who went down uncircumcised to the depths of the earth.” Elam was the kingdom of SW Iran (modern Khuzestan) that maintained semi-independence against Assyrian and Babylonian pressure for centuries. Ashurbanipal conquered and devastated Elam ca. 645 BCE; Elam was subsequently absorbed into the Babylonian and then Persian empire. The uncircumcised designation for Elam is accurate: Elamite burial practices and iconography do not show evidence of circumcision. “Bear their shame with those who go down to the Pit” — the shame (kalimmatam) is the public dishonor of dying by the sword rather than by natural causes, without proper burial rites.
25: “They have made her a bed among the slain with all her multitude” — the “bed among the slain” (mishkav betokh chalal) is the underworld sleep of the battlefield dead. Elam's repetition (the name appears twice in this passage) may reflect the complexity of Elam's history — it was conquered and reconquered multiple times, unlike the more straightforwardly fallen Assyria. The theological function of the repetition is emphasis: even an ancient and resilient empire like Elam, which repeatedly rose from conquest, is finally and permanently in the company of the dishonored dead. Its resilience only made its final fall more instructive.
26: Meshech and Tubal — “with all their multitude; their graves surround them — all of them uncircumcised, slain by the sword, though they spread terror in the land of the living.” Meshech and Tubal are peoples from Anatolia: Meshech (Musku in Assyrian records) is associated with the Phrygians of central Turkey; Tubal (Tabal) with peoples of eastern Anatolia. They appear as slave and bronze traders in Ezekiel 27:13, suggesting commercial prominence. They also appear as Gog's associates in Ezekiel 38:2-3, giving them an eschatological dimension beyond their historical identity. By Ezekiel's time they had been subdued by Assyria and later Babylon, making their presence in the Sheol gallery historical rather than predictive.
27: “Do they not lie with the fallen warriors of old who went down to Sheol with their weapons of war, their swords placed under their heads and their shields covering their bones” — this verse introduces a distinction within Sheol between the honored dead (“fallen warriors of old” buried with weapons) and the dishonored dead (the nations listed). Warrior burial with weapons is attested archaeologically across the ancient world: Mycenaean shaft graves, Canaanite warrior burials, Assyrian royal tombs at Nimrud — elite soldiers were buried with their swords. The “warriors of old” (giborey noflim me-olamim) are the ancient heroic warriors of legend who died honorably in battle and were buried with military honors. Meshech and Tubal, by contrast, are “uncircumcised, slain by the sword” — same violent death but no ritual honor.
28: “You too will be shattered among the uncircumcised” — the direct address to Pharaoh resumes, applying the Sheol gallery to the current oracle's subject. After the catalogue of fallen nations, Egypt is told: you will join them. The verb “shattered” (tishbar) is the same used for breaking Pharaoh's arm (Ezekiel 30:21-22) — the broken arm leads to the broken king in Sheol. Egypt will not receive the honor of the “warriors of old” but will lie in the shame category with all the uncircumcised nations listed above.
29: Edom — “its kings and all its princes — laid with those slain by the sword despite their might.” Edom was Israel's neighboring kingdom in the Negev/Transjordan region, descended from Esau. Edom's presence in the Sheol gallery is charged: Edom was condemned throughout the prophets for its assistance to Babylon during Jerusalem's fall (Psalms 137:7; Obad 11-14; Jeremiah 49:7-22; Ezekiel 25:12-14). “Despite their might” (bgiburatam) — Edom's fortress cities (Bozrah, Sela/Petra) were considered impregnable; Obadiah 3-4 addresses exactly this pride: “the pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rocks ... who say in your heart Who will bring me down?” YHWH brought Edom down along with all the others.
30: “All the princes of the north and all the Sidonians who have gone down with the slain — despite the terror they inspired, they are put to shame in their might” — the “princes of the north” (nesiey tzafon) are probably the Aramean and north Syrian rulers who had been Assyrian and Babylonian vassals. Sidon (the Phoenician city-state north of Tyre) fell to Babylon around 587-586 BCE, though the details are not fully documented. Sidon had been powerful enough to inspire terror — its navy and merchants dominated eastern Mediterranean trade. “Put to shame in their might” — the shame (veyevoshu miguvratum) echoes the Deuteronomic shame-formula (Deuteronomy 25:3); shame in Sheol is the permanent status of those who died without covenant honor.
31: “When Pharaoh sees them he will be comforted over all his multitude” — this is dark consolation: Pharaoh, arriving in Sheol to find Assyria, Elam, Edom, Meshech-Tubal, and Sidon already there, will be “comforted” (venecham) by the company. This is the bitter consolation that appears in Isaiah 14:9-11 for Babylon's king: “Sheol beneath is stirred up to meet you ... the worm is spread under you and worms cover you.” The “comfort” is real in a perverse sense: Pharaoh will not be alone in his shame, and will not be the worst or most disgraced of those in Sheol. Every great empire that preceded him arrived there first.
32: “For I spread his terror in the land of the living; yet Pharaoh and all his multitude will be laid among the uncircumcised with those slain by the sword, declares the Lord GOD” — the final verse completes the oracle with a theological summary statement. YHWH acknowledges that Egypt's terror in the land of the living was real — it was not imaginary or trivial. Egypt was genuinely powerful, genuinely terrifying to its neighbors. But the “yet” (venitan) is the theological pivot: earthly terror does not exempt from Sheol's equalizing judgment. The formula “laid among the uncircumcised with those slain by the sword” (yutan betokh arlim et-challey cherev) closes the oracle with the formula that has been the refrain of the entire Sheol gallery — Egypt joins the community of shame alongside every empire that claimed divine permanence and found only the Pit.
